did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses multi-scale deep ensemble learning, combining deep convolutional neural networks (EfficientNetB8, SEResNeXt101, DenseNet264) to detect melanoma skin cancer using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a 'multi-scale deep ensemble learning' pipeline for melanoma detection. It details specific methods (EfficientNetB8, SEResNeXt101, DenseNet264), datasets (SIIM-ISIC 2020), and reports new performance results (AUC 99.02%). Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE IRI), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multi-Scale Deep Ensemble Learning for Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2022 IEEE 23rd International Conference on Information Reuse and Integration for Data Science (IRI)
brief_summary: This study proposes a multi-scale deep ensemble learning pipeline for melanoma skin cancer detection using dermoscopy images from the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset and ISIC archives. The approach combines three pre-trained deep convolutional neural networks—EfficientNetB8, SEResNeXt101, and DenseNet264—utilizing techniques such as digital hair removal, Mixup and CutMix augmentation, and a multi-penalty regularization method. The models were trained on 32,000 images and evaluated on 3,126 test images using cross-validation. The proposed ensemble model achieved an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 99.02%, along with 98.85% accuracy, 98.83% specificity, and 96.17% sensitivity. These results demonstrate that the multi-scale ensemble approach outperforms several state-of-the-art algorithms in detecting melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the binary classification task. The dataset contains 2,584 malignant images (after augmentation) out of 35,126 total images. The study focuses on detecting melanoma skin cancer.
The negative class in the binary classification task. The dataset contains 32,542 benign images. These serve as the non-melanoma comparator for detection.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma (malignant) vs. benign skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to detect melanoma skin cancer using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign). The output involves probabilities for melanoma detection, but the task is defined as classification with metrics including Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and AUC. The dataset contains 32,542 benign and 2,584 malignant images. Future work mentions potential multi-classification, but the current study is binary.

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC 2020 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopy images of unique benign and malignant skin lesions obtained from more than 2,000 patients. The dataset was generated by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) and the Society for Imaging Informatics in Medicine (SIIM). Images originated from Barcelona Hospital, Vienna Medical University, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Australian Melanoma Institute, University of Queensland, and University of Athens School of Medicine. Malignant diagnoses were confirmed by histopathology, and benign diagnoses by expert agreement, longitudinal follow-up, or histopathology.

use.training: Used as the primary source for the combined dataset. 32,000 images from the combined dataset (derived from SIIM-ISIC 2020 and ISIC archive) were used for training.
use.validation: Used in cross-validation folds for model evaluation.
use.testing: 3,126 images from the combined dataset were used for testing.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: Additional images collected from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive to address class imbalance in the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset.

use.training: 2,000 malignant images were added to the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset to create a new combined dataset of 35,126 images (32,542 benign, 2,584 malignant). These were used for training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to train and evaluate the deep learning models, ensuring robust performance estimation by splitting the data into three folds.
Used as an ensemble learning strategy to create a variety of models trained on different subsets of the training data, combining their predictions to improve accuracy and reliability.
Used to aggregate the performance scores (AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy) across all cross-validation folds to report final results.
Used to reduce generalization error and improve performance by applying transformations (Horizontal Flip, Rotate 90 degrees) to test images and averaging the results.
Used to address the extreme imbalance between foreground and background class sizes during training, acting as a loss function rather than a statistical test, but influencing the statistical outcome.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

SIIM-ISIC 2020 + ISIC Archive.description: Combined dataset of 35,126 images (32,000 training, 3,126 testing) used for binary classification of melanoma vs. benign lesions.
SIIM-ISIC 2020 + ISIC Archive.notes: The proposed Multi-Scale Deep Ensemble Learning model achieved the highest AUC (99.02%) among the models tested in this study. It outperformed state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of AUC, though Takfarines et al. [5] reported higher Accuracy (99.33%) and Specificity (99.38%) in a previous study on a different dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-Scale Deep Ensemble Learning (EL) Model combining EfficientNetB8, SEResNeXt101, and DenseNet264
architecture_used.pretraining: All models were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset

regularization_techniques: Multi-Sample Dropout (duplicated dropout layers and subsequent layers)
architecture_details.pooling: Global Average Pooling (GAP) added to each model

training_details.learning_rate_scheduler: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Warm Restarts (SGDR) with aggressive cosine annealing
training_details.hardware: Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-7700HQ CPU 1.53 GHz and Nvidia GeForce MX150

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNN with Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) (Dina et al. [7])
CNN with segmentation and ANN (Pyingkodi et al. [8])
Hybrid CNN with Bald Eagle Search (BES) optimization (Gehad et al. [9])
preprocessing_techniques: Digital Hair Removal (DHR) algorithm to remove hair artifacts from dermoscopy images
Conversion of original-colored images to grayscale
Morphological blackhat filtering to find hair contours
Inpainting using a mask based on the fast marching method (FMM) to replace hair pixels
Image resizing to 512x512 pixels (also tested at 384x384 and 224x224)
Mixup data augmentation (random convex combination of raw inputs and label encodings)
CutMix data augmentation (replacing removed regions with patches from another image)
Test Time Augmentation (TTA) including Horizontal Flip and Rotate 90 degrees

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Data augmentation technique consisting of random convex combination of raw inputs and corresponding convex combination of one-hot label encodings. Formula: x̃ = λxi + (1 − λ)xj and ỹ = λyi + (1 − λ)yj, where λ ∈ [0, 1]. Used to reduce memorization of corrupt labels and increase robustness.

Data augmentation technique that replaces removed regions in an image with patches from another image, with ground truth labels mixed proportionally to the number of pixels. Formula: x̃ = M ⊙ xA + (1 − M) ⊙ xB and ỹ = λyA + (1 − λ)yB, where M is a binary mask and λ is the combination ratio.
Applied during inference to reduce generalization error. Two techniques were used: Horizontal Flip and Rotate 90 degrees. Transformed images are passed through the model and results are averaged.

Preprocessing/augmentation step to remove hair artifacts. Involves converting to grayscale, morphological blackhat filtering, thresholding, and inpainting using a mask based on the fast marching method (FMM). Allows models to focus on skin rather than hair.

conclusions: The proposed multi-scale deep ensemble learning pipeline, combining EfficientNetB8, SEResNeXt101, and DenseNet264, achieved high performance in melanoma detection with an AUC of 99.02%, accuracy of 98.85%, specificity of 98.83%, and sensitivity of 96.17%.
The proposed ensemble model outperformed several state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of AUC, including those by Zhang (91.70%), Dina et al. (97.10%), and Pyingkodi et al. (unreported AUC).

While the proposed model achieved the highest AUC among compared recent works, previous studies by Takfarines et al. and Pyingkodi et al. reported higher accuracy (99.33% and 98.32% respectively) and specificity (99.38% and 98.41% respectively) on different datasets.
The study demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed multi-penalty regularization technique and multi-sample dropout in improving model performance on the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset.
Techniques such as Mixup and CutMix augmentation, SGDR learning rate scheduling, Focal Loss, and Digital Hair Removal (DHR) were successfully exploited to improve detection performance.

The authors acknowledge that future work will involve extending the current binary classification framework to multi-classification features for other skin lesions such as basal cell carcinoma, vascular lesions, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and actinic keratosis.
important_remarks: The study combines the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset with 2,000 additional malignant images from the ISIC archive to address class imbalance, resulting in a combined dataset of 35,126 images (32,542 benign, 2,584 malignant).
Performance metrics (AUC 99.02%, Accuracy 98.85%) are reported as macro-averaged results across 3-fold cross-validation, which may not reflect performance on a held-out external test set.

The models were trained on a single GPU (Nvidia GeForce MX150) with an Intel Core i7-7700HQ CPU, which may limit reproducibility for larger-scale deployments compared to high-performance computing clusters.

The proposed ensemble model outperforms several recent state-of-the-art algorithms in AUC, but previous work by the same authors (Takfarines et al. [5]) reported higher accuracy (99.33%) and specificity (99.38%) on a different dataset.
The study acknowledges that future work is needed to extend the binary classification framework to multi-classification for other skin lesions (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, vascular lesions).

The dataset includes images from multiple international sources (Barcelona Hospital, Vienna Medical University, etc.), but the specific distribution of patient demographics or geographic bias is not detailed.
